skill.name: architecture
skill.description: Framework for architecture decisions, requirement discovery, trade-off analysis, pattern selection, and ADR documentation.
skill.summary: Guide architecture decisions with requirements, trade-offs, patterns, and ADRs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed in context and dismissed as false positives. The skill contains read-only architecture guidance, markdown examples, and ADR templates with no executable commands, network access, secret access, or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: The external command detections point to markdown code fences, tables, example documents, and skill reference links. No shell execution instruction or runnable command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain fenced markdown, documentation tables, and references to other skills. There is no executable shell syntax beyond markdown delimiters.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Architecture Terms
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography and Windows SAM detections map to ordinary architecture words, file descriptions, DDD guidance, OAuth, JWT, and SAML examples. They do not expose credentials or recommend weak cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens appear inside product architecture examples and pattern names. No cryptographic implementation or sensitive file path is present.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives for Reconnaissance Terms
low_findings.description: System reconnaissance detections refer to architecture pattern descriptions such as data sources, complex queries, and eventual consistency. No host inspection or environment discovery instructions are present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are decision-matrix content, not system enumeration. The skill only declares read, glob, and grep tool access.

content.value_statement: Architecture choices fail when teams skip context and trade-offs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through requirements, pattern selection, and ADR documentation.
actual_capabilities: Collects project context through scale, team, timeline, domain, and constraint questions.
Classifies projects as MVP, SaaS, or enterprise based on documented criteria.
Compares architecture patterns such as monoliths, microservices, DDD, CQRS, and event-driven design.
Produces ADR-style decision records with context, options, rationale, trade-offs, and revisit triggers.
Provides reference examples for MVP, SaaS, and enterprise architecture decisions.
Highlights common over-engineering anti-patterns and simpler alternatives.
limitations: It provides guidance and templates, not executable architecture automation.
It does not inspect repositories beyond read-only context gathering.
It does not replace expert review for regulated or safety-critical systems.
It depends on accurate user input about scale, team, timeline, and constraints.

use_cases.title: Plan an MVP Architecture
use_cases.description: Choose a simple architecture that matches a small team, short timeline, and limited initial scale.

use_cases.title: Document SaaS Technical Decisions
use_cases.description: Turn competing options into ADRs that explain constraints, trade-offs, and revisit triggers.

use_cases.title: Review Enterprise Pattern Choices
use_cases.description: Compare distributed system patterns against team size, domain boundaries, scaling needs, and operational cost.

prompt_templates.title: Start Architecture Discovery
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new project
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architecture skill to ask the minimum context questions before recommending an architecture for my product.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Pattern Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architecture skill to compare monolith, modular monolith, and microservices for my project constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Write an ADR
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architecture skill to write an ADR for choosing PostgreSQL, including options, rationale, trade-offs, and revisit triggers.

prompt_templates.title: Challenge an Existing Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the architecture skill to review this proposed system design, identify over-engineering risks, and recommend simpler alternatives.

output_examples.input: We are building a marketplace MVP with one developer and eight weeks.
output_examples.output: The skill would classify the project as MVP, recommend a simple full-stack architecture, and list migration triggers for later complexity.

output_examples.input: Our SaaS team is debating modular monolith versus microservices.
output_examples.output: The skill would compare team size, domain boundaries, scaling needs, and operational cost before recommending a pattern.

output_examples.input: Create an ADR for adopting an event-driven workflow.
output_examples.output: The skill would produce an ADR narrative with context, considered options, decision rationale, accepted trade-offs, consequences, and revisit conditions.

best_practices: Start by answering scale, team, timeline, domain, and constraint questions.
Ask for simpler alternatives before accepting a complex pattern.
Record every significant decision as an ADR with revisit triggers.
anti_patterns: Choosing microservices before domain boundaries and scaling needs are proven.
Adding repositories, CQRS, or DDD to simple CRUD systems without a clear reason.
Writing architecture decisions without documenting trade-offs and constraints.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps structure architecture decisions using requirements discovery, trade-off analysis, pattern guidance, and ADR documentation.

faq.question: Does it generate production infrastructure?
faq.answer: No. It provides decision guidance and documentation templates, not deployable infrastructure or executable automation.

faq.question: Can it help choose between monoliths and microservices?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares team size, domain boundaries, scaling needs, and operational complexity before recommending a direction.

faq.question: Is this useful for small projects?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes simple architecture for MVPs and adds complexity only when constraints justify it.

faq.question: Does it include ADR support?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes ADR structures for context, options, decision, rationale, trade-offs, consequences, and revisit triggers.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
